response: The EU’s GDPR, in effect since May 2018, reshaped personal data handling by applying to any company processing EU residents’ data, regardless of location, and granting rights such as access, erasure, portability, and explicit consent. It requires some organizations to appoint Data Protection Officers, mandates breach reporting within 72 hours, and allows fines up to 4% of global annual revenue or €20 million, whichever is higher. Major penalties include Meta’s €1.2 billion fine in 2023, Amazon’s €746 million fine, and Google fines totaling over €200 million. Although criticized for compliance burdens, user-frustrating cookie banners, and inconsistent enforcement, GDPR has influenced global laws like California’s CCPA, Brazil’s LGPD, and India’s data protection law, establishing privacy as a fundamental right.
